# Proposal for Circle Founders Club Korea Branch Leadership & Structure

Rahul Yadav proposes to lead the establishment of a Korea branch of The Circle Founders Club (TCFC). This brief outlines the opportunity landscape of Korean startup grant programs, the strategic value of a local entity, a compliant compensation plan for Rahul, and options for structuring the Korea branch. The goal is to secure TCFC leadership buy-in to hire Rahul as Korea Branch Lead with a base salary + bonus (in lieu of commissions) and to permit setting up a Korean-registered entity to represent TCFC in local programs.

## I. Korean Grant Opportunity Landscape (2024–2025)

South Korea offers multiple government-backed programs to support startups’ global expansion. Key programs include the **K-Startup Centre (KSC)**, **Global Acceleration Program** (GAP/GAPS), and **Global TIPS**, each with distinct application processes, eligibility, and benefits:

### K-Startup Centre (KSC) Program 
- **Purpose:** Enables Korean startups to **enter, settle, and scale in global markets** by partnering them with overseas accelerators【16†L991-L1000】【5†L157-L165】. Cohorts are run in target countries (e.g. India, France, Israel, Singapore) with local accelerator partners. 
- **Application Process:** The Ministry of SMEs and Startups (MSS) and KISED announce a call on the K-Startup portal (typically Q1)【35†L155-L164】. **Steps:** (1) **Project Notice** is posted【35†L137-L145】; (2) **Application & Document Screening** – startups submit a business plan and any required forms for their chosen accelerator/country【35†L167-L170】; (3) **Interviews** by KISED and the partner accelerator【35†L139-L147】; (4) **Pre-Program (Bootcamp)** – a 1-week industry-focused program with mentoring and localization guidance, after which a final evaluation selects the startups moving forward【35†L114-L122】【35†L139-L147】; (5) **Regular Program** – 6~10 week overseas acceleration with business matching, expert mentoring, and meetups in the host country【35†L120-L128】; (6) **Follow-on Support** – post-program check-ins and support such as help with incorporation or fundraising【35†L147-L152】.
- **Responsible Departments:** MSS (Global Growth Policy Division) sets policy and funding, and KISED (Global Startup Division) implements the program【35†L171-L179】. KISED coordinates with selected **global accelerators** (e.g. HEC Paris, The Circle FC in India) to operate the cohorts【16†L991-L1000】【5†L157-L165】.
- **Eligibility Requirements:** Korean startups (typically <7 years old) that **have secured investment or a recommendation letter** from credible sources (accelerators, VCs, or large firms)【35†L103-L110】. This ensures participants have some traction or endorsement. Additional documents include a business plan and any forms required by the specific accelerator/country【35†L167-L170】.
- **Evaluation Criteria & Timing:** Selection focuses on the startup’s global expansion potential, product-market fit for the target country, and capability to localize. Initial screening happens shortly after the application deadline (e.g. spring), and final cohort selection occurs post-bootcamp (mid-year). *Example:* In 2020, applications ran March–May【35†L155-L163】. In 2024, the France cohort started July 15【16†L1013-L1021】, and the India cohort ran for 4 months culminating in a Demo Day by November【5†L129-L137】【5†L153-L162】.
- **Grant Value & Cohort Size:** The KSC program is large in scale (e.g. ₩38.5 billion budget supporting ~72 startups in a cycle)【35†L108-L116】. Each startup gets a small **“commercialization fund”** during the pre-program (~₩5M) and can receive up to **₩80M** in an **Overseas Expansion Fund** to cover expenses like travel, local hiring, marketing, patenting, or incorporation abroad【35†L125-L133】. The cohort size per country is around 5–15 startups; for instance, **10 Korean startups** were in the K-Startup Centre India 2024 cohort【5†L153-L161】. Overall, about 60–80 startups per year may benefit across all country cohorts. Each selected accelerator (partner) typically hosts one cohort annually.

### Global Acceleration Program (GAP/GAPS) 
- **Purpose:** Helps Korean startups **test and localize their products in overseas markets** by matching them with established accelerators for short-term programs【2†L97-L105】. It serves earlier-stage companies looking for initial global exposure.
- **Application Process:** KISED opens a call (via K-Startup portal) for startups to apply to specific global accelerator tracks【2†L177-L184】. **Steps:** (1) **Application Submission** by the startup to KISED, indicating target country/accelerator【2†L139-L147】; (2) **Assessment & Selection** – a **document review and interview** conducted *in partnership with the chosen overseas accelerator*【2†L143-L151】 (the accelerator provides input to select startups that fit its program); (3) **Orientation & Pre-Mentoring** – selected startups receive preparatory mentoring (market research, expert advice) before going abroad【2†L152-L159】; (4) **Overseas Acceleration** – a ~6-week intensive program in the host country with the accelerator’s incubation, mentoring, and networking【2†L114-L122】; (5) **Result Evaluation** – the accelerator (with KISED) evaluates outcomes at program end (e.g. business progress, local traction)【2†L158-L164】.
- **Responsible Departments:** MSS (Global Growth Policy Division) oversees policy, and KISED (Global Startup Division) manages execution【2†L185-L193】. KISED coordinates with multiple **partner accelerators** worldwide (e.g. Plug and Play in Silicon Valley, InnoSpace in Shanghai, HEC Incubator in Paris, etc.)【2†L128-L136】 to run the acceleration modules.
- **Eligibility Requirements:** Korean **pre-founders or startups under 7 years old** are eligible【2†L104-L111】, ensuring the program targets early-stage ventures. There is no requirement of prior investment for GAP; even very young startups can apply. Applicants must prepare a project plan tailored to the target accelerator’s market and any requested materials【2†L177-L184】. 
- **Evaluation Criteria & Timing:** Selection is based on the startup’s technology/product readiness, fit with the target market/accelerator’s focus, and team’s capacity. Each global accelerator partner may have input on companies that match their domain (e.g. fintech, AI, etc.). The application calls are typically announced via **public notice** on K-Startup; for example, a call in early 2024 led to programs running in the summer/fall. Due diligence via document scoring and video interviews leads to a decision within a few weeks of application closing【2†L143-L150】. 
- **Grant Value & Cohort Size:** The program has a total budget of ~₩3.4 billion, supporting **around 60 startups** annually【2†L108-L115】. Each selected startup is granted up to **₩10 million** in government support【2†L119-L127】. This grant covers participation fees for the accelerator program and related costs (e.g. local marketing or IP expenses). Typically ~10 startups are placed per country/accelerator track (for example, cohorts in U.S., China, UK, France, Vietnam, etc., collectively totaling ~60)【2†L128-L136】【2†L108-L115】. The ₩10M is often a reimbursement for expenses, so startups must document their spending on approved items to receive the funds.

### Global TIPS Program 
- **Purpose:** **Extends Korea’s flagship TIPS program globally** by supporting startups that have secured foreign venture investment to expand overseas【14†L70-L78】【14†L87-L95】. It merges TIPS’s public-private co-investment model with an emphasis on international market entry.
- **Application Process:** MSS issues a call for **startups with foreign VC backing** to apply for Global TIPS (first launched in 2024)【14†L70-L78】. **Steps:** (1) **Application & Proof of Investment** – startups must show they have raised at least **USD $200,000 from an overseas VC** fund【14†L70-L78】 (evidence like investment contracts); (2) **Proposal Evaluation** – an expert panel evaluates the startup’s **global expansion plan**, business feasibility, strategies for further funding, and overall team capability【14†L98-L101】; (3) **Selection of Winners** – only ~20–30 companies are selected per cohort (20 in the 2024 inaugural batch)【14†L98-L102】【34†L267-L274】; (4) **Funding Agreement** – each selected startup enters an agreement to receive the matching funds and is connected with **“globally specialized” partner institutions** for support【14†L102-L110】【34†L269-L277】; (5) **Project Execution** – over a period (up to 3 years), the startup leverages funds for overseas subsidiary setup, product localization, hiring, etc., while MSS monitors progress through those partner institutions.
- **Responsible Departments:** The program is driven by MSS (Startup Venture Innovation Office)【14†L112-L118】 with implementation support likely from KISED or Korea Venture Investment Corp, aligning with the broader TIPS framework. MSS also designates **global accelerator/VC partners** (“globally specialized operators”) to mentor and network the startups internationally【34†L269-L277】. This public-private approach is a hallmark of TIPS.
- **Eligibility Requirements:** The key criterion is securing **≥ $200K from foreign investors** *before* applying【14†L70-L78】. This ensures the startup already has international investor validation. Companies must be Korean tech startups (usually meeting standard TIPS age/stage criteria – generally early-stage, under 7 years). Many applicants already have global connections; indeed, in 2024, 12 of the 20 selected had investment from funds backed by Korea’s own Fund of Funds【14†L98-L102】. Participants are expected to use the grant for activities directly tied to global expansion (market entry, overseas R&D, local office, etc.), so they should have a clear expansion project plan.
- **Evaluation Criteria & Timing:** Competition is intense – **118 companies applied for 20 spots in 2024**【14†L98-L101】. Judges assess the viability of each startup’s global market entry strategy, product competitiveness internationally, and plans to secure additional funding abroad【14†L98-L105】. They also consider the strength of the foreign investor involvement and the startup’s capability to manage an overseas operation. The selection was announced in August 2024【14†L59-L67】, implying applications and evaluations took place over summer. For 2025, MSS is expanding the intake to **30 startups**【11†L9-L17】【34†L267-L274】, with likely similar timelines (applications mid-year, selection by Q3).
- **Grant Value & Support Structure:** Each Global TIPS startup is eligible for up to **₩600 million** (~$480K) in government matching funds【14†L71-L75】【14†L102-L110】. This funding is typically disbursed in tranches over a multi-year period (up to 3 years)【14†L102-L110】 as the startup meets milestones in their expansion plan. The money can be used for establishing overseas subsidiaries, product localization, hiring, marketing, or other costs of entering a foreign market【14†L87-L95】. In effect, the government is matching the commitment shown by foreign VCs. Selected startups are also plugged into **expert support networks** – MSS assigns them to specialized partner organizations (which could be international accelerators or consulting firms) that provide mentorship, introduce local partners, and help manage the logistics of going abroad【14†L102-L110】【34†L269-L277】. This combination of funding and hands-on support is meant to ensure companies successfully penetrate global markets【14†L111-L118】. Cohort size has been small (20 in 2024), making this a **highly selective program**.

**Summary:** *These programs represent significant non-dilutive funding and resources for TCFC’s startup portfolio or cohorts. By engaging with KSC and GAP, TCFC can bring Korean government resources to startups it accelerates (e.g. hosting Korean startups or sending companies abroad), while Global TIPS presents opportunities to support later-stage teams in scaling out of Korea. Understanding the steps and criteria of each program is crucial for TCFC to successfully secure and manage such grants.* 

## II. Value of Having a Korean-Registered Entity

Establishing a legal entity in Korea (e.g. a locally registered accelerator or consulting company) will **greatly enhance TCFC’s ability to participate in and benefit from the above programs**. For each phase of engagement, a local presence provides key advantages:

- **Credibility in Applications:** Korean government programs often favor or require local ties. A Korea-registered accelerator can be officially recognized and build trust with MSS/KISED. For example, in 2024 KISED only recruited **accelerators with business registration in the target region** to run global programs【24†L113-L118】. By the same token, TCFC having a **registered entity in Korea** signals commitment and makes it easier to partner on Korean initiatives. It shows TCFC is not just a foreign entity dropping in, but a stakeholder in the local ecosystem, which can improve selection odds in competitive programs.
- **Eligibility & Program Compliance:** Some grants **mandate local registration to receive funds** or act as an operator. Government funding is typically disbursed to Korean bank accounts and requires local tax invoices/receipts. If TCFC were to administer program funds (e.g. as an accelerator partner or contractor), a domestic entity is needed to **sign contracts and legally receive grant money**. For instance, to be a formal *창업기획자* (Accelerator) partner in TIPS or K-Startup programs, an organization must be registered in Korea or formally recognized by a Korean authority. A local company can also more easily hire Korean staff or interns under these programs (some grants reimburse personnel costs but only if the entity is local).
- **Operational Advantages (Banking & Reimbursement):** A Korean entity can open **local bank accounts** in KRW, simplifying transactions. Government programs often work on a reimbursement model: the entity or startup spends funds on approved expenses and then submits receipts to claim the grant. Having a local corporate account and the ability to issue **tax receipts (세금계산서)** streamlines this process. It avoids cross-border payment delays and currency issues, enabling TCFC to **quickly receive grant payments and pay vendors** (venues, service providers) in Korea. Essentially, a local entity acts as a financial conduit that is recognized by Korean authorities, which is critical for smooth fund flow.
- **Local Billing & Invoicing:** If TCFC needs to **invoice MSS/KISED for services** (say, operating an accelerator cohort or consulting on a program), a domestic entity is far simpler. Korean government offices generally cannot pay invoices to foreign companies without complex procedures. A local entity can **bill in Korean won with proper tax codes**, and thus get paid promptly for any program-related contracts. Similarly, it can **issue receipts to startups** for any fees (complying with Korean accounting), which may be required for startups to get their grant reimbursements. All these back-office functions are more straightforward with a registered local company.
- **Better Partnership & Networking:** Being on-the-ground allows TCFC to **forge relationships with Korean ministries, incubators, and corporates**. A local entity can join industry associations, sign MOUs with government agencies, and be invited to official networking events. This proximity and formal status can open doors – for example, attending KISED briefings, or co-hosting events during **K-Startup Week ComeUp** (Korea’s big startup festival【2†L51-L59】). It solidifies TCFC’s brand as a participant in Korea’s startup ecosystem, not just an overseas partner. Over time, this can lead to inside knowledge of new programs and policy changes, giving TCFC a competitive edge in securing grants.

Moreover, certain programs literally **require a Korean corporate presence** for startups to fully benefit:
- **Grant Disbursement Rules:** Programs like KSC and GAP fund Korean startups’ overseas activities, but the startups themselves remain Korean entities that must report to MSS. If TCFC sets up as a Korean accelerator, it can **formally host these startups** (e.g. as an “Accelerator under Article 24”, see below) and be a direct beneficiary of government support (subsidies for operating costs, etc.). Some grant schemes will only reimburse expenses if incurred through a Korean-registered organization. For example, if TCFC Korea runs an accelerator program, costs like venue rental, consulting, or travel stipends for startups could be covered by government funds *only* if invoiced by a domestic entity. 
- **Program Requirements:** **Global TIPS**, while focused on startups, is planning to designate “globally specialized operators” to support companies【34†L269-L277】. A TCFC Korea entity could position itself to be such an operator, but likely only if it’s registered and recognized by MSS. Similarly, future initiatives (e.g. **Korean “Soft Landing” centers for foreign startups, or inbound accelerators**) usually insist on local incorporation for those running the program. Being ready with a Korean entity ensures TCFC doesn’t miss these opportunities.

**Local Incorporation Options & Costs:** TCFC has two main pathways to establish itself in Korea:
- **Standard Corporation (주식회사):** This is the simplest form – essentially a private limited company. It can be 100% foreign-owned and requires **minimal start-up capital** (no formal minimum; a token capital like ₩1–5 million is common for a small consultancy). The incorporation process takes a couple of weeks and costs only a few million KRW in government fees and legal charges. This entity can engage in all accelerator and consulting activities (sign contracts, hire employees, etc.). However, by itself it is not yet a government-recognized “accelerator” in the legal sense.
- **Registered Accelerator (창업기획자) – Article 24 Registration:** For added credibility, the local entity can register with the Korean government as an official startup accelerator. Under the **Venture Investment Promotion Act**, an organization can apply for this status (often referenced as registering under *Article 24* of the Act). To qualify, the company must meet stricter criteria – **typically at least ₩50 million in paid-in capital** and **at least 2 full-time professionals on staff** with relevant experience (these are legal requirements for accelerator registration)【30†L1-L4】. It must also designate a compliance officer and file periodic reports. The registration process can take a few months. Once approved, TCFC would be a certified accelerator in Korea’s startup ecosystem. This status can be valuable: it allows the entity to form **investment funds (individual investor associations)**, to co-invest in startups with government matching, and to apply for programs specifically open to registered accelerators (such as TIPS operator selection, certain MSS grants, etc.). It essentially embeds TCFC Korea deeply into the government’s framework for startup support. **Cost:** Aside from the ₩50M capital (which remains the company’s working capital), additional costs are relatively low – some application fees and administrative overhead – but the need to maintain payroll for at least 2 qualified staff is a consideration.

- **Lower-Bound Setup Costs:** For a basic setup (주식회사 without accelerator reg), expect **under ₩5M ($4K)** in one-time costs (incorporation paperwork, stamps, etc.) and perhaps ₩20–30M ($16–25K) annual running cost for a small office and minimal staff. By contrast, pursuing accelerator registration effectively raises the initial commitment (due to the ₩50M capital and hiring requirements), but these funds are not “spent” – capital can be used for operations, and hiring could be scaled as needed (e.g. one could hire two staff at modest salaries to meet the requirement). In either case, these costs are small relative to the potential **grant inflows** TCFC could access (each successful program can bring in tens or hundreds of millions KRW in support).

In summary, a Korean entity provides **legitimacy, operational ease, and compliance**. It acts as the essential bridge for TCFC to fully leverage Korean grants – improving the chance of winning projects, and ensuring efficient execution of those projects (financially and legally). Without a local entity, TCFC would face administrative hurdles, miss certain funding opportunities, and possibly violate local regulations (e.g. around tax or fund flows). Thus, establishing a Korea branch/company is a strategic investment that would quickly pay off in successful program participation.

## III. Employment Proposal for Rahul – Salary + Performance Bonus (No Commissions)

**Role & Justification:** Rahul Yadav will serve as the **Korea Branch Lead** for TCFC, spearheading grant program partnerships and operations. Given his experience and past salary (approximately ₩60M annually), we propose a compensation structure that is cost-effective for TCFC yet strongly incentivizes performance, while **avoiding any compliance pitfalls** associated with commission-based pay.

- **Base Salary:** Set a modest fixed salary at or near **Korean minimum wage** levels – for instance, around **₩2.0–2.5M per month** (roughly ₩24–30M per year). This could even be structured as a part-time salary initially (to conserve resources until programs are secured). A base in this range provides Rahul basic financial stability (and meets legal minimums for a full-time position) but is significantly lower than his previous ₩60M, demonstrating his commitment to a performance-based model.
- **Performance-Linked Bonuses:** Implement a **milestone bonus scheme** tied to tangible successes in Korea. Rather than a percentage “commission” on grant amounts, bonuses will be flat or pre-defined amounts triggered by key achievements:
  - *Program Win Bonus:* If Rahul successfully helps TCFC secure a **program contract or grant** (for example, TCFC is selected as an official accelerator partner for K-Startup Centre or another MSS program), he receives a bonus of a certain amount (e.g. ₩X million) for each program secured. This rewards the effort of writing proposals, networking, and winning competitive bids.
  - *Cohort Success Bonus:* For running programs, Rahul can earn bonuses based on **outcomes** (e.g. each startup in a cohort that receives funding or each cohort that completes successfully could yield a bonus). For instance, if a KSC India-type cohort is executed under TCFC Korea and meets its targets, a bonus could be paid at Demo Day completion.
  - *Funding Milestone Bonus:* If TCFC Korea or its startups achieve significant **grant disbursements** due to Rahul’s facilitation (for example, ₩X hundred million secured across programs in a year), an escalating bonus can reward that scale. This aligns with TCFC’s goal of maximizing grant revenue.
- **Why Not Commissions:** It’s important to **avoid a direct commission model** (e.g. “**Y% of any grant money**” as Rahul’s pay). In South Korea, **contingency fees for securing public funds are legally sensitive**. Government grants are taxpayer money, and giving a middleman a cut can be seen as unethical or even unlawful. The **Improper Solicitation and Graft Act** (김영란법) imposes strict limits on payments related to public officials or funds – a commission arrangement could be interpreted as an improper inducement if any lobbying of officials is involved. Even aside from legalities, from TCFC’s perspective, paying a percentage of grants could create perverse incentives or public relations issues (appearing as if TCFC “sells” government money). By using a flat salary+bonus, TCFC ensures compliance and transparency – Rahul is rewarded by the company for performance, **not by skimming off government payments**. This structure is internally driven and thus does not violate anti-graft rules, since the bonus is a predetermined reward from TCFC to its employee, not a finder’s fee from the grant itself.
- **Compliance & Alignment:** A fixed salary plus bonuses tied to **legitimate performance metrics** keeps everything above-board. There’s no element of paying for influence – Rahul’s bonuses are for work done (like any performance bonus) and can be documented as part of his employment contract. This is fully compliant with labor laws and avoids any suggestion of fee-for-favor. It also aligns Rahul’s interests with TCFC’s long-term goals: he has a personal stake in securing programs and ensuring they succeed (to earn bonuses), but he also has a stable base pay that encourages him to build sustainable operations (not just chase one-off deals at all costs). 
- **Illustrative Numbers:** If Rahul’s base is ₩30M and, say, he brings in two major programs in 2025, TCFC could grant him a ₩10M bonus for each. That yields a total compensation of ₩50M – still below his old salary, yet if he exceeds targets, bonuses could raise it above ₩60M. This flexibility means TCFC **only pays big salaries when corresponding value is delivered**, keeping fixed expenses low. As the Korea branch grows, the base can be reviewed (for example, after a year of proven success, TCFC might raise his base closer to market rate, reflecting increased responsibility).
- **Long-Term Incentives:** Beyond immediate bonuses, TCFC can consider **equity or profit-sharing** in the local entity for Rahul if appropriate (especially in a JV scenario – see next section). This would further motivate him to develop TCFC Korea’s business. However, the primary focus in the first 1-2 years should be cash performance bonuses to directly tie into grant outcomes.

In summary, the proposed structure (low base + milestone bonuses) ensures **compliance, motivation, and fairness**. It prevents any conflict with anti-corruption laws that a commission model might trigger, and it reassures TCFC leadership that Rahul’s incentives are perfectly aligned with the club’s success in Korea. Essentially, *Rahul only “earns big” when TCFC does too*, creating a win-win scenario.

## IV. Entity Structure Options for TCFC Korea

There are several ways to establish TCFC’s presence in Korea. Below we outline three viable models, each with its pros, cons, compliance considerations, and an estimated timeline:

- **Option 1: Rahul’s Own Entity + MOU with TCFC**  
  *Description:* Rahul incorporates a **Korean company under his ownership** (for example, “Circle Korea Consulting, Inc.”) to act as the local vehicle. TCFC (the parent organization in India) signs a **Memorandum of Understanding (MoU)** or partnership agreement with this Korean entity, granting it rights to represent TCFC in Korea (use of brand, executing programs, etc.). Essentially, Rahul’s company becomes TCFC’s affiliate/partner in Korea.  
  *Pros:* This is **quick to set up** – a basic company can be registered in a matter of weeks. TCFC avoids the bureaucratic process of foreign direct investment registration since Rahul (an individual) handles local incorporation. It gives Rahul autonomy to act fast and pivot as needed on the ground. TCFC incurs minimal upfront cost (no need to inject capital; perhaps it can fund Rahul’s entity via the programs once they are won). The MoU can be flexibly written and even non-exclusive.  
  *Cons:* TCFC **lacks direct ownership or control**. The arrangement relies on trust in Rahul’s stewardship. There’s a risk of misalignment – e.g. if priorities diverge, TCFC’s recourse is limited to the MoU terms. Branding and quality control need to be enforced contractually (to ensure Rahul’s entity upholds TCFC standards). Additionally, some Korean officials or partners might perceive the entity as a third-party contractor rather than “TCFC itself,” which could slightly dilute the brand impact. Investors or startups might prefer dealing with an official branch of TCFC rather than a partner company.  
  *Compliance:* Legally, the Korean company is a separate entity – **liability stays with Rahul’s company**. TCFC Global isn’t automatically liable for local debts or legal issues (which can be a pro). However, reputation risk remains: any compliance lapse by the local entity (e.g. misuse of grant funds or breach of contract) would reflect on “TCFC” due to the branding connection. The MoU should include compliance clauses to mitigate this. Also, if the MoU gives the local entity a commission-like fee from TCFC for any grants, that must be structured carefully to avoid graft implications (better to stick to TCFC paying the local entity for services or the local entity taking agreed portions of program budgets as the operator). As long as the local entity abides by Korean laws (filings, taxes, etc.), this model is sound.  
  *Timeline:* **2–4 weeks** to register a new corporation (with Rahul as representative director). Drafting and signing the MoU could be done in parallel, taking maybe **1–2 weeks** of negotiation. Overall, within a month or so, this structure can be in place. Rahul’s company could then immediately start bidding for programs (even showing the MoU to demonstrate TCFC backing if needed).

- **Option 2: TCFC-Owned Local Branch or Subsidiary**  
  *Description:* TCFC (the parent club) directly establishes a presence in Korea, either as a **branch office of the foreign company** or a **new Korean subsidiary company** that is majority or wholly owned by TCFC. In either case, TCFC is the owner, and Rahul would be hired as the local branch manager or subsidiary CEO.  
  *Pros:* This model gives TCFC **full control and ownership** of the Korean operations. The entity would formally be “The Circle Founders Club Korea” (or similar), which is powerful for branding – Korean partners will recognize it as the direct Korean arm of TCFC. Decision-making can be aligned easily with HQ, and profits or funds flow directly to the parent. It also simplifies internal accounting (Korean branch results roll up into TCFC). Trust and alignment are high, as Rahul operates as an employee/manager under TCFC’s oversight.  
  *Cons:* Initial setup is a bit more involved. Registering a foreign branch requires paperwork from the parent company (authenticated documents, etc.) and the branch isn’t a separate legal entity (the parent bears liability for branch actions). Setting up a subsidiary (a new Korean company owned by TCFC) means TCFC must invest capital (technically as foreign direct investment) and go through incorporation – which can take slightly longer due to FDI report filings. There is also the **commitment of capital** – while not huge, TCFC might need to put in, say, ₩10–50M as capital for the new subsidiary to fund its operations. Management of compliance falls on TCFC – they will need to ensure all Korean regulations are followed, requiring some knowledge of local law (though Rahul can handle day-to-day compliance). Additionally, profits from a subsidiary, if any, need formal dividend repatriation to the parent, though grants are mostly non-profit revenue anyway.  
  *Compliance:* A subsidiary is a separate legal entity (limited liability), which can be good – TCFC’s liability is capped to its investment. A branch, however, is not separate – TCFC HQ would be directly liable for any debts or lawsuits, which is riskier. In either case, having TCFC as owner means **strong internal controls** can be implemented from day one. TCFC can set global policies on anti-corruption, financial reporting, etc., and enforce them in the Korea office. Korean law will treat the entity as foreign-invested, but beyond some extra reporting, it’s straightforward. One compliance consideration: to register certain programs (like becoming an official accelerator or handling government contracts), the entity may need a Korean national as an employee or certain licenses – but these are manageable. Overall, this model is fully compliant and likely viewed positively by Korean authorities (they appreciate FDI and serious foreign players establishing local offices).  
  *Timeline:* **4–8 weeks** for setup. A branch office might be on the shorter end of that (since it skips creating a new corporation, but involves registration with the tax office as a foreign company branch). A subsidiary will require an FDI report to KOTRA or a bank (can be done in days), incorporation registration (1–2 weeks after paperwork is ready), and then getting business registrations (tax ID, etc.). With good counsel, it can be done in about a month. Realistically, allow up to two months to account for document shipping, apostilles (for parent company docs), and any unexpected delays. Once established, hiring Rahul and transferring him to a proper work visa (if needed) might add a few weeks, though if he’s already resident this is simpler.

- **Option 3: Joint Venture (TCFC + Rahul)**  
  *Description:* TCFC and Rahul co-found the Korean entity with a **shareholding split** – e.g. TCFC owning 51% and Rahul 49%, or vice versa. This JV company would be “TCFC Korea” in branding, but its shares are divided. It marries TCFC’s capital and brand with Rahul’s local leadership and equity stake.  
  *Pros:* **Shared investment and risk** – TCFC doesn’t have to fund 100% of the operation; Rahul (and potentially other local partners, if desired) would contribute some capital or sweat equity. Rahul having equity (especially if he’s a significant minority or majority holder) gives him a strong **personal incentive** to grow the venture’s value. He is not just an employee, but a co-owner, which can be motivating and aligns long-term interests. TCFC, by retaining a majority (if 51/49 with TCFC 51), still maintains control while benefiting from the commitment of an entrepreneur partner. Externally, Korean stakeholders will still view it as TCFC’s local arm (especially if TCFC is majority shareholder), so brand integrity is maintained. Internally, the equity structure could be designed to vest Rahul’s shares over time, ensuring he stays for the long haul.  
  *Cons:* Setting up a JV requires **negotiating a detailed shareholders’ agreement** – covering governance, profit split, future investment, exit clauses, etc. This is more complex than either of the above options. There is a possibility of future disagreements between TCFC and Rahul as co-owners (hence the need for clear agreements on decision-making powers, deadlock resolution, etc.). If Rahul holds majority (51%), TCFC might worry about being a minority in its own brand’s entity – that could be mitigated by supermajority voting requirements or contractual controls, but it’s a consideration. On the other hand, if TCFC holds majority, Rahul might feel less empowered than expected as a “partner”. So the 51/49 split must be chosen thoughtfully based on trust and strategic control. Financially, splitting profits might not be a big issue early on (grants typically don’t generate profits, mostly covering costs), but if the entity starts generating revenue (e.g. consulting fees or equity exits), the division of returns should be agreed upfront.  
  *Compliance Risks:* A JV is a Korean corporation, so from a legal standpoint it’s similar to Option 2’s subsidiary in complying with local laws. One risk is if roles are not clearly defined – e.g. who is legally the representative director? Likely Rahul, since he’s local. If TCFC is majority, they’ll want certain oversight (like board control). All these must be clearly documented to prevent any governance disputes that could affect compliance (for example, if there’s disagreement on reporting to MSS or handling of funds, it could jeopardize grants). But if structured well, a JV can actually enhance compliance: Rahul’s intimate knowledge of local norms combined with TCFC’s institutional policies can create a robust governance framework. Another consideration: under Korean law, if one shareholder goes rogue or insolvent, it can affect the company – so both parties need to maintain good standing. Also, foreign ownership processes apply for TCFC’s share, similar to Option 2. No special permits are needed just because it’s a JV, beyond what a normal company requires.  
  *Timeline:* Similar to a wholly-owned subsidiary, about **4–8 weeks**. The additional step here is negotiating and drafting the JV agreement and articles of incorporation – that could be done concurrently with incorporation paperwork. If negotiations are smooth (since both parties are already inclined to work together), it might not add much delay. However, prudence suggests spending some time (a few weeks) to iron out the agreement. So, an optimistic timeline is one month; a cautious timeline is two months to launch the JV. 

**Comparison & Recommendation:** All three options are feasible. **Option 2 (TCFC-owned subsidiary)** gives the most control and clear branding, which TCFC’s leadership may prefer for a long-term strategic presence. **Option 1** is very nimble and low-commitment – it could even be a temporary solution (Rahul’s entity acts as a placeholder until a TCFC subsidiary is ready). **Option 3** strikes a balance, locking in Rahul’s commitment via ownership while keeping TCFC deeply involved. 

From a compliance standpoint, Options 2 and 3 ensure TCFC can directly enforce its standards, whereas Option 1 relies on contract enforcement. Financially, Option 1 and 3 reduce TCFC’s initial outlay (Rahul shoulders some load), whereas Option 2 means TCFC funds it all but also reaps all benefits. 

One possible approach is a **phased strategy**: start with Option 1 for speed – Rahul sets up his entity and begins engagements in Q1 2025 under an MoU. As soon as TCFC sees traction (e.g. first program secured), transition to Option 2 or 3 by mid-2025 – formally incorporate TCFC Korea, with an arrangement to fold Rahul’s entity or have him join the new entity (maybe even give him a small equity or profit share for his efforts). This way, TCFC can test the waters with minimal risk and then commit structurally once value is proven.

**Timeline to Execution:** 
- *Immediate (1–2 months):* Decide on structure and begin entity setup. If Option 1, Rahul’s company could be operational by next month. If Option 2 or 3, initiate incorporation paperwork now to have TCFC Korea established by, say, February 2025.  
- *Short-term (3–6 months):* Pursue grant program applications (KSC 2025, Global TIPS 2025, etc.) under the new entity. By mid-2025, ideally secure at least one program. Use this period to also register as an official accelerator if decided (that process can run in parallel; it might complete by mid-year).  
- *Long-term (6–12 months):* Evaluate performance and adjust structure if needed (e.g. if started with Option 1, incorporate TCFC-owned entity and smoothly transfer operations). Ensure all compliance filings (tax, potentially accelerator registration by end of year) are done. By the end of 2025, TCFC should have a fully functioning Korean branch/entity, led by Rahul, with one or more grant programs under its belt.

---

**Conclusion:** This proposal demonstrates a clear plan to leverage Korea’s rich startup support programs by installing Rahul Yadav as TCFC’s Korea Branch Lead, supported by a compliant incentive structure and a local legal entity. By investing in a Korean presence, TCFC stands to unlock substantial grant funding (potentially hundreds of millions of KRW) and strategic partnerships, far outweighing the modest setup and salary costs. The recommended approach balances risk and reward – giving Rahul the motivation to drive success, while protecting TCFC’s interests and adhering to Korean laws. With leadership’s approval, we can initiate this plan immediately and position The Circle Founders Club as a prominent accelerator in Korea’s thriving startup ecosystem in 2025 and beyond.

metadata.title: KISED: Korea Institute of Startup & Entrepreneurship Development | HEC Paris
metadata.text: The K-Startup Center Program, initiated by the Ministry of SMEs and Startups (MSS) and facilitated by the Korea Institute of Startup and Entrepreneurship Development (KISED), aims to support Korean startups in entering the global market. Through partnerships with global accelerators(Incubateur HEC Paris), the program provides resources and office space in target countries, facilitating the scale-up of Korean startups. MSS is responsible for SME, venture company, and startup policies, while KISED supports startups through education, funding, and global expansion initiatives, making it a leading institution in Korea for tech-based startup commercialization.

metadata.text: The South Korean Ministry of SMEs and Startups (MSS) has selected 20 companies for its ‘Global TIPS‘ initiative, designed to support startups that have secured at least $200,000 in investments from overseas venture capitalists. These startups are eligible to receive up to KRW 600 million (approximately $480,000) to aid their international expansion efforts.

metadata.title: Korea's MSS Announces 20 Startups for Global TIPS International Expansion Program - WOWTALE
metadata.text: This year’s introduction of ‘Global TIPS’ extends the existing program’s strengths—merging private pre-investment and governmental support—to the global stage. It specifically facilitates international expansion by supporting activities like the establishment of overseas subsidiaries, moving beyond traditional domestic export assistance.

MSS is responsible for SME, venture company, and startup policies, while KISED supports startups through education, funding, and global expansion initiatives, making it a leading institution in Korea for tech-based startup commercialization.

content_references.title: Korea's MSS Announces 20 Startups for Global TIPS International Expansion Program - WOWTALE
content_references.snippet: The South Korean Ministry of SMEs and Startups (MSS) has selected 20 companies for its ‘Global TIPS‘ initiative, designed to support startups that have secured at least $200,000 in investments from overseas venture capitalists. These startups are eligible to receive up to KRW 600 million (approximately $480,000) to aid their international expansion efforts.

content_references.title: Korea's MSS Announces 20 Startups for Global TIPS International Expansion Program - WOWTALE
content_references.snippet: This year’s introduction of ‘Global TIPS’ extends the existing program’s strengths—merging private pre-investment and governmental support—to the global stage. It specifically facilitates international expansion by supporting activities like the establishment of overseas subsidiaries, moving beyond traditional domestic export assistance.
